political.s: Walmart's PAC donated $2.1M in the 2024 FEC cycle with a bipartisan split (roughly 58% Republican, 42% Democrat based on $12,500/$21,500 ratio data), consistent with its posture as a large employer seeking access across the aisle. OpenSecrets confirms the company spent $7.24M on federal lobbying in 2024 and $3.89M in total cycle contributions, focusing on trade, tax, and regulatory issues. The company avoids ideological alignment and prioritizes policy access over partisan loyalty.

charity.s: Walmart and the Walmart Foundation contributed $2 billion in cash and in-kind donations globally in FY2025, making it one of the largest corporate philanthropists in the world. The company's Spark Good platform consolidates local grants, customer round-ups, and volunteer programs, with focus areas including hunger relief, economic opportunity, sustainability, and community resilience. Walmart also deployed over $23 million in hurricane relief during 2024.
charity.focus: Hunger relief, economic opportunity, sustainability, racial equity, disaster relief

environment.s: Walmart has set a net-zero emissions target for global operations by 2040 without relying on carbon offsets, and achieved its Project Gigaton goal of reducing 1 billion metric tons of supply chain emissions six years ahead of schedule. In 2024, nearly 48.5% of global electricity came from renewable sources, and operational emissions fell 18.1% from the 2015 baseline. However, Walmart acknowledges it will miss its 2025 interim emissions target and Scope 3 emissions remain a significant unresolved challenge.

labor.s: Walmart has a persistent record of OSHA violations and has paid hundreds of thousands in fines since 2016, and was listed on the National COSH 'Dirty Dozen' employer list in both 2019 and 2024. The NLRB found Walmart violated the National Labor Relations Act by restricting employees from discussing discipline and retaliating against striking workers in multiple states. With a median U.S. worker wage of approximately $29,469–$32,000 annually and heavy reliance on part-time labor, associates report inadequate safety measures, workplace injury risks, and limited upward mobility.

dei.s: Walmart made a major reversal on DEI in late 2024, ending its $100M Center for Racial Equity commitment, withdrawing from the Human Rights Campaign Corporate Equality Index, and dropping race and gender as supplier contract criteria. The rollback followed pressure from conservative activist Robby Starbuck and drew backlash from over 30 shareholders representing $266 billion in assets and 13 state attorneys general. Walmart rebranded its DEI language to 'belonging' while stating internal employee policies have not changed.

animals.s: Walmart is a retailer, not a cosmetics or pharmaceutical manufacturer, and does not conduct animal testing on products. The company does have animal welfare supplier policies requiring cage-free eggs and sow housing standards aligned with the Five Freedoms framework. As a food and general merchandise seller, direct animal testing is not applicable to its core operations.

guns.s: Walmart sells long guns (hunting rifles, shotguns) in approximately half of its U.S. Supercenters but has significantly curtailed its firearms business over the years. The company stopped selling AR-15s and military-style rifles, ended handgun sales (except Alaska), raised the minimum purchase age to 21, and banned open carry in stores as of 2019. Walmart requires background check approval before any firearm sale, exceeding federal law which permits sales after a 3-day waiting period even without a completed check.

privacy.s: Walmart is one of the largest private data collectors in the U.S., sharing consumer data with more than 50 third parties through its apps and websites and collecting data on an estimated 145 million Americans. Critics and advocacy groups have raised concerns about data sharing practices, lack of transparency on algorithmic use, and Walmart's lobbying against consumer privacy legislation. While Walmart offers opt-out mechanisms and adheres to CCPA and GDPR frameworks, its data monetization practices and retail media network raise significant privacy concerns.

execPay.s: Walmart CEO Doug McMillon's total compensation reached approximately $27–30M in recent fiscal years against a median employee wage of roughly $27,000–$32,000, producing a CEO-to-worker pay ratio of approximately 930:1 (FY2024 proxy) to 469:1 (FY2026 proxy depending on methodology). The extreme ratio reflects Walmart's large part-time and international workforce which suppresses the median wage figure. Shareholders approved the pay package with 96.3% support, though labor advocates and worker groups have consistently criticized the disparity.

primaryOffenses.category: off-label or unapproved promotion of medical products
